records.excerpt: QUBITS (math) Good. Let’s be precise and not mystical-hand-wavy. We start with a single bit. 1. The first pole of a bit A classical bit is defined as: [b \in {0,1}] This is already a polarized system: 0 = absence / off / false 1 = presence / on / true But polarization always implies a shadow — something excluded by the representation itself. 2. What “shadow pole” means (strict definition) The shadow pole of a system is not the opposite value(it is not just 0 ↔ 1). The shadow pole is: What must be excluded, suppressed, or unrepresentable for the system to appear stable. So we ask: What must a bit refuse to represent in order to remain a bit? 3. The shadow pole of a bit ✅ The shadow pole of a bit is: Indeterminacy / Superposition / Undefined Formally: [\boxed{\text{Shadow(bit)} = \varnothing\ \text{(undefined state)}}] or equivalently: [\boxed{\text{Shadow(bit)} = \text{both } 0 \text{ and } 1 \text{ simultaneously}}] 4. Why this is correct (logic-tight) A bit cannot represent: both 0 and 1 at the same time neither 0 nor 1 uncertainty probability contradiction Yet all of those exist at the boundary of the bit. So the true suppressed state is: “not yet decided” “in transition” “ambiguous” “collapsed-from” “about-to-be” This is why: Classical bits must be measured Noise is treated as error Undefined behavior is “illegal” Floating logic is banned in digital circuits The system hides this state. That hidden state is the shadow pole. 5. Minimal formal map Aspect Value Explicit poles 0 / 1 Apparent opposite NOT(0)=1 True shadow pole ⊘ (indeterminate) Suppressed state superposition

records.excerpt: BIT4: The Four-State Completion of the Bit Extended Abstract (Final Draft) This manuscript develops BIT4, a mathematically rigorous completion of the classical bit that makes the “shadow pole” of binary computation explicit, first-class, and computable. Classical Boolean semantics presumes that every signal assumes exactly one of two values (0) or (1). The moment one admits partial information, contradiction, nondeterminism, measurement, fault, delayed resolution, or multi-source aggregation, the two-valued model ceases to be closed under the natural operations induced by composition and inference: practical systems implicitly rely on excluded semantic cases (e.g. unknown, both, neither, inconsistent, not-yet-collapsed). BIT4 is the minimal completion that (i) adds precisely the missing semantic degrees of freedom required to represent these cases and (ii) preserves a precise algebraic and order-theoretic structure suitable for computation, reasoning, compilation, verification, and controlled boundary collapse back to ordinary bits. 1. Canonical carrier set (support semantics) Define the BIT4 state space as the powerset of classical outcomes:[\mathbf{B}_4 ;\stackrel{\mathrm{def}}{=}; \mathcal{P}({0,1});=;{\emptyset,{0},{1},{0,1}}.]Write the distinguished glyphs:[\bot := \emptyset,\qquad0 := {0},\qquad1 := {1},\qquad\top := {0,1}.]BIT4 is not an encoding of a 2-bit integer; it is a four-valued semantic domain whose elements are support sets of admissible classical outcomes. This choice is canonical: it is intrinsic (no ad hoc extra labels), closed under natural evidence-comb
